C[ygramul: This rengo thing works less smoothly than I thought 😅
C[Tom Newman: By less smooth do you mean your partner is dragging you down?  I feel like I'm way out of my league! Interesting for me and hope fully not too frustrating for everyone else.

Tom Newman: It's confusing to me to go back and see who played what moves.  When I try to catch up on the sequence since my last move I'm not sure who played what. Probably not that important to the current state of a game but something for feedback
Tom Newman: Does anoek every play rengo?
ygramul: Oh no no! I didn't mean it like that!

ygramul: What I was thinking is the issue that many people don't take the game very seriously and they just forget about the game and timeout. This cuts the rythm of the game, that doesn't really start until the only players that remain are the ones willing to play
ygramul: The issue that when it's your turn you have to review the last moves and maybe you won't understand their logic... I guess is part of the fun? 🤷‍♂️🤷‍♂️
C[Tom Newman: I agree it's interesting to come back to the game and catch up. It forces some more review and reconsideration.
Tom Newman: What is confusing is correlating moves to the players
Tom Newman: Maybe shorter times would help. Can you set up tournaments for this? Maybe something like bridge where 4 players play 3 games and each game with a different partner?
Tom Newman: Then the winner of the match is who has the most wins and they advance?
C[ygramul: I don't think you can set up rengo tournaments 🤔🤔
ygramul: At least I don't remember seeing the option, but I have not created that many tournaments
C[Tom Newman: I haven't seen it either. I'm sure @anoek could figure it out!
C[Tom Newman: Sorry to see this end by timeout.
Tom Newman: Happy to try again if you ever need an extra player.
ygramul: Thank you all for participating
bp7: Noooo, I'm so sorry, what a pity.
bp7: I think that last move was losing for me
ygramul: Anyway, it's difficult to find a consistent group of players. In all the rengo games I've played most people timeout or leave the game very soon
ygramul: I was in another game that ended up being a two against two, and my partner just left without saying anything... the internet, I guess 😅

ygramul: pero respondo en un orden raro xD

ygramul: 2) Definitivamente sí. La ventaja de negras por jugar primero está cuantificada en entre 5 y 7 puntos. De ahí que en el go moderno (siglo XX) blancas reciba unos cuantos puntos adicionales, que se llaman komi. En el caso de nuestra partida, yo tengo 6.5 puntos desde el inicio, para nivelar tu ventaja como negras. Vi unas estadísticas una vez que reflejaban que antes de establecer el komi negras ganaba bastantes más veces que blancas (creo recordar que era un 56-42), y después de poner komi los números se habían equilibrado (creo recordar que eran 52-48 para negras)
ygramul: La iniciativa en go es súper fundamental. Un jugador de negras que la mantiene tiene mucho ganado, mientras que uno que la pierde sabe que ha cometido un error severo.
ygramul: Anda, me acabo de dar cuenta de que ya he respondido a la pregunta 3! xD

ygramul: 1) La verdad es que no lo sé, pero sospecho que blancas perdería porque, al tener negras la iniciativa, en algún momento podría matar a un grupo de blancas antes de que ese mismo grupo mate a negras.
ygramul: Piensa, de todas maneras, que la simetría del go es radial, no bilateral. Para seguir esa estrategia de la que hablas has de jugar el simétrico respecto del punto central (llamado tengen)

ygramul: Yo vi una partida en la que negras renunciaba a la iniciativa a cambio de jugar tengen en la primera jugada, y a partir de ahí negras copiaba a blancas en todas las jugadas. En esa partida, blancas le proponía una competición a negras alrededor del tengen: básicamente ambos se estaban rodeando mutuamente, y el primero que cerrase el círculo mataba al otro, y por ende ganaba la partida. Blancas llegó antes, porque negras le había cedido la iniciativa
ygramul: No sé si ésa es una técnica general que se puede aplicar a cualquier caso de imitación, habría de pensarlo...
ygramul: en fin, muevo ya! jajaj
gilram: y como había yo analizado esto...
ygramul: quiere decir que no te volverá a pasar, :-)

ygramul: Este tipo de estructura, como la negra que empieza en Q15, se suele llamar ""escalera"". La única manera de escapar a la captura es que haya una piedra negra en medio del recorrido de la escalera (digamos, en S12). Si lleva a la pared o a una piedra blanca, es captura segura.

ygramul: And they also do fancy things with sound like you 😂

C[masterps: There's a quite a lot of stuff in air acoustics solids and water are a bit behind. Solids normally gets done with geography since you can use the same principles for things like earthquakes, water is just a bit annoying to be honest 😂
C[masterps: It's dense enough it affects how the sound interacts at the boundary so you can't make certain assumptions
masterps: Also the attenuation in water is ridiculous, you go into the hundreds of kHz and the sound travels very little distance before becoming having next to no amplitude

ygramul: As for the Roman influence on Spain: maybe the most important point is the language. Spanish is derived from Latin, so most of the words we use come directly from the language of the Romans

ygramul: Related to this, there are also many many place names directly derived from Latin. Some of them in a rather funny way. There's this city called Zaragoza which was initially called Caesaraugusta because it was founded by the first Roman emperor, Caesar Augustus. But in the coming centuries the people began to shrink the name, from Caesaraugusta to saraugussa (roughly) and from there to the modern Zaragoza. That happens in many places in Spain

ygramul: Another Roman legacy are the codes of law. In Spain the laws are descended from the Roman laws via a very crooked path. We didn't preserve the Roman laws through the Middle Ages, but rather Spain became divided into the Christian realms and Muslim Al-Andalus. The Christian kingdoms were for several centuries very poor and essentially forgot the Roman way of making laws. In Al-Andalus, for their side, they had Islamic Law, so they didn't care about the laws of the Romans. But in the East, in Constantinople, one Roman emperor, Justinian, commissioned a group of lawyers with compiling all Roman laws up to that moment and peeling off any contradictions that they could find (because the early Roman emperors used to issue laws without looking if someone else had already written a law about that). The result is called the Code of Justinian or, in Latin, the Corpus Iuris Civilis, and was compiled around the year 530. The Corpus continued to be used by the Romans in the Byzantine Empire until 1453, when the Turks conquered Constantinople. It was partly remembered in Europe during the Middle Ages, but not in Spain. Greek immigrants coming to the West after the fall of Constantinople brought copies of the Code and from that point it was more widely known and appreciated. But it definitely came to Spain thanks to... Napoleon Bonaparte. The Napoleonic Code of Laws was heavily based on the Code of Justinian, and when Napoleon conquered half of Europe he brought the Roman laws with him. Spain is one of the countries which retained that tradition and has nowadays Roman-style laws
ygramul: I had written a very long explanation about Roman law and now it is lost :(
ygramul: Okay, so the short version: in Spain, as in other European countries, our laws are modeled according to some principles that come directly from the Romans

ygramul: An example of this: in England (and the United States) the decision of a judge can become a pseudo-law. They are called ""precedents"", and the idea is that if a judge considered a case similar to your case, the decision of that first judge should take precedence. The logic is that all judicial decisions should be consistent, and so the decision of a judge 200 years ago can be important for a case today

ygramul: In Spain it doesn't work like that. The laws are written by the Parliament, and the judges must interpret them, but if two judges make a different interpretation that's not a problem. The logic here is that the judicial system has an absolute reference, which is the law written by the Parliament, and every judge must try to interpret that reference in the most consistent way
ygramul: This last logic is the Roman logic, as opposed to the English one, which comes from the Germanic tradition
ygramul: But actually the Roman tradition was lost in Spain for many centuries, and it only came back with Napoleon Bonaparte 200 years ago

C[nobhaete: So in Western Europe, the Roman law system back after the medieval times.  How about Eastern Europe?  Russia called itself the 'Third Rome', did it influenced by Roman law that strongly? And how about Ottoman? Turks also said they successor of East Rome.
ygramul: So, these three questions have different answers, but essentially the reasons why the Russians and Turks claimed to be successors of Rome are not very much related to law

ygramul: I don't know a lot about Russian history, but if I'm not mistaken it was a rather feudal state (so with many semi-independent nobles) until at least the 1700s. From that moment it transformed into an absolute monarchy in which the crown was inherited strictly by blood

ygramul: None of those features are very much 'Roman'. The Romans considered the monarchy as an 'office', something that was in the service of the people. That's why so many generals and pretenders were successful in taking the bug job for themselves. In Rome your blood right was a factor, but not the only one.

ygramul: Apart from that, Russia implemented a system of serfdom from the 1600s which is also very much un-Roman. A serf is a peasant who is bonded to the land, and can be sold and bought together with the land. A serf is not a slave, but is rather close... That would be unthinkable in the Roman system

ygramul: I think the major claim that Russia holds to the succession of Rome is Orthodox Christianity. That's the flavour of chrisitanity that developed in the Roman Empire and spread across most of Eastern Europe. With tha fall of Constantinople, Russia became the most important orthodox polity, and inherited quite many Roman customs in religion. Even today, the Patriarch of Moscow and the Patriarch of Constantinople (who still exists and resides in Istanbul) argue about who is ""the most important patriarch of Orthodoxy""
ygramul: So in religious and cultural terms... yes, Russia can be regarded as heir to the Roman Empire. In political and law affairs I don't think so

ygramul: As for the Turks, they developed a multi-ethnic, multi-language, multi-religious empire that ineed reminds us of the Roman Empire of the old times. And incorporated into their bureaucracy some features of the late Roman (Byzantine, I mean) state. But again, their laws and customs are very different
ygramul: Ottoman law stemmed from Islamic law, which is very very different from Roman law. And in the Ottoman state this idea that ""the emperor is a servant of the people"" also disappeared
ygramul: (of couse, many Roman emperors did not behave as servants of anybody, but still, the idea that the office of emperor answered to the Roman people remained until the end)
ygramul: So the Ottomans may look like Rome in some social and administrative aspects, but again the laws and many political ideals were very different

ygramul: I guess we are left to admit that when Constantinople was conquered by the Turks the Roman political and legal system effectively disappeared as such. It would be later ""revived"" in Western Europe, especially by Napoleon Bonaparte and the states that maintained the Napoleonic Code, but that was ""inspired by Rome"", rather than ""a continuation of Rome""
